title: Edit Distance
description:
Edit Distance

Given two strings word1 and word2, return the minimum number of operations required to convert word1 to word2.

You have the following three operations permitted on a word:

  • Insert a character
  • Delete a character
  • Replace a character

Examples:

  • Input: word1 = "horse", word2 = "ros"
    Output: 3
    Explanation: horse → rorse (replace 'h' with 'r') → rose (delete 'r') → ros (delete 'e')
  • Input: word1 = "intention", word2 = "execution"
    Output: 5
  • Input: word1 = "", word2 = "abc"
    Output: 3
    Explanation: Insert three characters.

Constraints:

  • 0 <= word1.length <= 500
  • 0 <= word2.length <= 500
  • word1 and word2 consist of lowercase English letters
solution_text:
Edit Distance

Algorithm (2D Dynamic Programming):

  1. Build a DP table dp where dp[i][j] = minimum edits to convert word1[:i] to word2[:j]
  2. Base cases: converting any prefix to an empty string costs as many deletions as its length; converting an empty string to any prefix costs as many insertions as its length
  3. Transition: if characters match, dp[i][j] = dp[i-1][j-1]; otherwise take 1 + min(replace, delete, insert)

Time Complexity: O(m × n) where m and n are the lengths of word1 and word2

Space Complexity: O(m × n) for the DP table (reducible to O(n) with a rolling array)

Key Insights:

  • This is the classic Levenshtein distance algorithm, foundational to spell-checkers and diff tools
  • When characters match, no operation is needed — we inherit the cost from the diagonal cell
  • The three operations map directly to three adjacent cells: replace (diagonal), delete (above), insert (left)
